text: A video essay by a YouTuber still sticks with me in emphasizing just how poetic and smart the writing of the movies are. Based on the rules that govern the secret society of assassin in John Whick, the YouTuber compares characters like John and Winston to Greek gods and Greek mythology. I think it was Film Cow. Either way, the best way to appreciate the writing and interactions between characters and their motivations, is to rewatch over again to view the symbology underneath. I’m more curious of what will be left of John after it’s all over? Will he be able to go back to being the peaceful man that strived to grieve and move on? Or will the violent life be left behind be all that consumes his thoughts after being excused from the High Table?

I was 16 years old when the first Top Gun came out, seeing this was like catching up with old friends I had lost touch with for 36 years.

text: "Even simply brushing clothes with someone is fate" ( ) is an old Korean proverb that means every interaction between you and another person—even a stranger on the street—is never an accident, but rather the elaborate result of your past lives and theirs intertwining to make that momentary interaction happen. And if even the most seemingly trivial of interactions are the result of elaborate workings of fate ( ), then something as significant as meeting the love of your life is the result of 8000 of fate. This is a commonly known Buddhist teaching. (Gup) is a measurement of time. Imagine a massive boulder, and imagine that a single raindrop falls on that boulder once every 1000 years. The amount of time it would take for the boulder to finally crack from the water droplets is 1 . A single is an unfathomably long period of time, so 8000 is something that's hard to even attempt to imagine. The saying goes that for every married couple, there's at least 8000 of (fate) from their past lives that led them to each other.

text: Hey, I'm from South Korea and I can relate to this trailer... Almost same thing happened to me recently. Around 17 years ago, when I was in elementary, I met a boy whose name was "Present" in Korean. He was my first crush. I liked his shiny round eyes, a cute wide smile, and white teeth. Sadly, I had to move to another school a year later. 18 years passed, and we met each other this January. We have become both 25. He studies in medical school and I just came back to S.Korea after obtaining a diploma from another country. I didn't know he had been reading all my posts on my public diary for a few months when I was back in France. Though he knew the one who was writing the diary was me, he never said "Hi" to me. Then one day I deleted a link that had access to my public diary, so he missed a chance to let me know. A few years passed, and he noticed one comment in the comment section of one youtube video which showed a whole view of our hometown where we used to play together. That comment was, surprisingly, written by me. He noticed that it was me because I wrote down some of my personal background in the comment. He already knew I was living in France and I had a very special surname that stood out. So we met each other once again through Youtube and we talked hours and hours on social media. We finally met each other this January and we had a nice Italian lunch, drank coffee and talked for hours and hours... I think we had a nice time. We are not together tho. Maybe it was because of a bad timing or maybe because I've been having some issues with my mentality and physical health or maybe because he was in senior year at medical school...or maybe because I seemed such a deep person to him. BUT the important thing is, I have a feeling that we are still connected and one day we will see each other once again at right time and a right place. Magic happens to those who believe in magic :) Anyways, thank you for reading my magical story

text: Reconnected with my love after almost 10 years apart this past fall in NYC (I’m thirty). Coincidentally, the first time we saw each other we also ended up in Brooklyn and took pictures in the same places they are taking pictures in the movie with the bridge in the background. Circumstances took her across the world. I always knew she was the one and on starry nights, red sunsets, during milestones and in silent moments of reflection, I would wonder if I was still in her heart as she was in mine. When we finally met up, I remember gazing into her eyes for hours searching for answers, we said so much to each other without saying a single word. Turns out, I have been in her heart as much as she’s been in mine. 3 months later this past January I travelled to meet her family. We’re hoping to get engaged mid year and planning a wedding for early 2024. Timing is everything and in our hearts and in our core, we always hoped to be each others forevers. Update: We got married in Feb 2024

text: - In-yun - Destiny, providence, fate. - In-nen in Japanese. " " or " " - Inn-Yuen in Chinese I love, love, love this. I think in the idea of In-yun is the yearning of all humans. That there be something more. something more than just *this*. that the simple and amazing world out there, so amazing that even the so simple mundane unmentioned unnoticed brief encounter of two strangers contain multitudes of magic and meaning and timefilled weight. That there be a real light out there in the dark, beyond the brief flickers of each star's burning fires. Our every forward motion is a romanticization of our existence and our living, and with religion we cling to this limited crawling. I think I have gotten to a root of why I love religion. Because I love life too, I romanticize life too. I love the ways in which peoples of the past and present have taken this love and forged complex mythologies, philosophies, theologies, and societies. The love of life being the founding force and motivation to create such masterpieces as the Hebrew Bible, the New Testament, the Mahabharata, the Quran, on and on and on and on. To love life is to need, desperately, to conceptualize it, understand it, and hope to find some way to maybe keep it, that somewhere hidden between the grains of dust on our lonely planet is some indication that the dead live on, in a place we cannot yet see or imagine. Life is too good. It is too good. It is so good that even in our sorrows we know it to be good. Even in our anguish we relish in how good life is, marvel at how amazingly good it is. With some understanding that even our sorrows are the products of a complex immense beautiful silent programming that hums under the soil of everything, and which weaves tapestries with the stones and sticks of this realm- writes epics in the sand. Takes each mortal by the hand and guides them through moments strung together into a mundane masterpiece of life. That is why the Hebrews write their creation myth with Adam and Eve being cursed to mortal woes. That God promises the first humans of the Israelites that their every moment will be filled with sorrow. We cling so hard to this thing. Even in imperfection and pain. Our souls placed in unfirm forms, our mortal flesh fractured from time to time by the tides and days. Imperfect constructs of tissue and chemicals, but pulsing fierce, a yell into the silence. Defiance, the unconscious call of our shells. We are all together holding on. The days are so much smaller now, i live so much less, i feel so much less powerfully. But in this moment I feel something really powerful. And so I write and write all of this. What I feel is so heavy, so heavy, and melancholic. I cannot describe the feeling, in truth however. Because even though these characteristics I give are genuine and accurate and something others experience regularly, this feeling is unknowable. The description is actually so feeble. Its as though each Day of my past had been turned into a person by a spell. A person I love. And then each person was killed in front of me. These people-days massacred. This is the past. The past is slaughtered as the future replaces it. It hurts. It weighs on me. Its all leaving me behind, these past things. Because I know the past is always gone I know the future will be gone too. It'll all go. It'll all go. I've watched this same trailer for "Past Lives", like 5 times now. Each time with this feeling, this weight. This great great feeling. All these descriptions still not anything close to what it is. Its an abstract thing. It doesn't pertain to the realm of describable things. Everytime I watch this trailer I come back and write some more. I haven't had this feeling in a long time. This melancholy. There I go again with a description. One more thought. The child that does not yet understand that objects may remain permanent, but just out of view - is the man writing his theology. That first moment when the child does not see that which it once knew, and weeps, though the thing only hides just a small ways off, is the man in desparate need of religion. This tiny naive moment contains our defining characteristic, the primeval instinct to fear the loss of it All, of everything. We eventually learn that objects no longer seen may reasonably still exist, just out of our view. But the exact form of that very first fear, the only fear, is with us forever. Things no longer existing. *us*. no longer existing. Man inevitably creates theology, and then never lets it go. He cannot.

text: I believe that In-Yun has its roots in Chinese, specifically in the words " " or " ," both of which mean fate. The latter emphasizes its romantic aspect, and they are both pronounced "Inn-Yuen." I always love the similarities among Eastern Asian cultures. There is a concept in Chinese similar to what you mentioned that states, " ," which means "Glimpse but once, a past life's glance. Five hundred times, to find the chance, To cross paths with one from before, In this life, and forevermore.". Or " ", meaning "Ten lives of good deeds earn a shared boat ride. A hundred more, a shared pillow at your side.".
raw_text: I believe that In-Yun has its roots in Chinese, specifically in the words "因缘" or "姻缘," both of which mean fate. The latter emphasizes its romantic aspect, and they are both pronounced "Inn-Yuen." I always love the similarities among Eastern Asian cultures. There is a concept in Chinese similar to what you mentioned that states, "前世五百次的回眸，换来今世的一次擦肩而过," which means "Glimpse but once, a past life's glance. Five hundred times, to find the chance, To cross paths with one from before, In this life, and forevermore.". Or "十世修来同船渡，百世修来共枕眠", meaning "Ten lives of good deeds earn a shared boat ride. A hundred more, a shared pillow at your side.".

text: Hey guys, thank you so much for sending such encouraging messages to me. I was so glad to share this magical story with the world. Unfortunately, this story ends with no magic. I have recently found out that he had a girlfriend during that time and still, he is going out with this lady. How did I know about his secret love then? I asked him out for a Japanese animated movie filmed by his favorite movie director Shinkai Makoto but then he confessed that he had a girlfriend and his girlfriend wouldn't like him to go out with me to watch the movie He later told me he didn't confess this secret on the day we met because he didn't want to draw a line between me and him. He said it would be okay to go out, eat, and talk together but it would be not okay to watch a movie together. Umm... I didn't know how to respond but I just wished him luck because he was and still my first love. I felt disappointed that he had lied to me and so depressed that I got to know his secret in April when cherry blossoms bloom. But now, I am actually okay because I have realized that...no matter what he does and says, he was and still my first love. I still love him and thank him for having created such wonderful childhood memories with me in Korea. I still wish he could become a good doctor and lead a beautiful life with his girlfriend/wife. I believe this is what we call 'unconditional love'. I'm finally having surgery this month, and I hope it goes well unlike how it went with my childhood crush. Anyways, thank you for sending so much love and support towards me. I wish you guys best of miracle Love, Seeun
raw_text:
Hey guys, thank you so much for sending such encouraging messages to me. I was so glad to share this magical story with the world. Unfortunately, this story ends with no magic. I have recently found out that he had a girlfriend during that time and still, he is going out with this lady. How did I know about his secret love then? I asked him out for a Japanese animated movie filmed by his favorite movie director Shinkai Makoto but then he confessed that he had a girlfriend and his girlfriend wouldn't like him to  go out with me to watch the movie😅 He later told me he didn't confess this secret on the day we met because he didn't want to draw a line between me and him. He said it would be okay to go out, eat, and talk together but it would be not okay to watch a movie together. Umm... I didn't know how to respond but I just wished him luck because he was and still my first love. I felt disappointed that he had lied to me and so depressed that I got to know his secret in April when cherry blossoms bloom. But now, I am actually okay because I have realized that...no matter what he does and says, he was and still my first love. I still love him and thank him for having created such wonderful childhood memories with me in Korea. I still wish he could become a good doctor and lead a beautiful life with his girlfriend/wife. 
I believe this is what we call 'unconditional love'. 

I'm finally having surgery this month, and I hope it goes well unlike how it went with my childhood crush. 

Anyways, thank you for sending so much love and support towards me. I wish you guys best of miracle💞💞

Love, 
Seeun

text: Stephen King has explored the different types of fear a writer can inspire within his audience in detail; horror and terror being the two main categorisations. Horror is the fear of a real threat, which can be something grotesque such as a decapitated head or giant spiders, or violent and disturbing imagery. Terror on the other hand, is the fear of something which by nature poses no threat, such staring into the darkness and waiting for something to appear. Terror is the anticipation of the event, Horror is the event itself and the release of that tension. This allows the writer to play off of the individual fears of each member of their audience as the fear comes from their own imagination. Terror goes somewhat hand in hand with a third category, known as ‘the error’. This is looking out of your window and seeing a small child dressed in Victorian clothing looking back at you. Children pose no threat, but it is the fact that it is out of place in that context which makes it terrifying. The same reasoning applies to why killer clowns are scary, a clown is meant to entertain children, but is actually vicious killer. This reversal of expectations, this ‘error’, is what makes them seem so especially despicable. So in short, whilst you might not find jump scares effective, they do fall into the category of horror as they an example of an imminent threat and release of tension. These categories don’t only apply to books, but films too; when you are next watching a scary film, keep these categories in mind and try and spot which you find most effective :))) hope you enjoyed reading

text: I somewhat disagree (about your statement regarding jump scares). Lemme ask you this: If you plan on scaring a friend or family member, what's the first prank that comes to mind? For most people, I would argue it's jump scaring. And you wouldn't do it every day, right? It'd be once in a while, when they least expect it. You'd do your best to keep them from seeing it coming, hold your breath, camouflage yourself, etc. That's how movie jump scares can be effective. If they're used sparingly, in the right moment, and it's always best to try to keep it as unpredictable as possible. One EXCELLENT example of a good, tactical jump scare that nobody saw coming, was the opening scene of "The Cabin in the Woods." Another would be the "sloth" scene in "Se7en," and another during the blood test scene in "The Thing" (1982), or the dream-telling scene in "Insidious", really I could go on. Jump scares can DEFINITELY be "good" horror, if used correctly. I believe horror shows/movies should use jump scares like they should use CGI: Rarely, thoughtfully, and in the right moments. Essentially, NOT as a crutch ("The Thing" [2011] I'm looking at YOU).

text: The claim that "whoever says this film is boring or not good does not understand true horror" is both presumptuous and incorrect. It assumes that the critic's understanding of horror is flawed simply because they do not appreciate a particular film. However, horror is a broad and diverse genre, and dismissing differing opinions with such absolutism reveals a shallow understanding of what truly constitutes horror. In reality, true horror is not confined to any one stylistic approach, and those who blindly reject others' perspectives may themselves lack a nuanced comprehension of the genre. The comment also suggests that "jump scares are not horror," which is an oversimplification. While it is true that horror should not rely solely on jump scares, they remain a legitimate and effective tool when used correctly. A well-placed jump scare can heighten tension, surprise the audience, and contribute to an atmosphere of dread. Classic horror films such as Jaws (1975) and The Exorcist (1973) skillfully incorporate jump scares without diminishing their psychological terror. To dismiss an entire technique within the genre is to ignore its versatility and impact when executed with precision. Furthermore, true horror is about evoking fear, dread, and unease. While some films achieve this through slow-building tension and psychological depth, others use visceral imagery, eerie sound design, or shocking moments to elicit fear. What one person finds terrifying, another may find dull. This subjective nature of horror means that no single definition can be universally applied. A film that fails to engage or scare a viewer cannot simply be defended by claiming the critic lacks an understanding of horror; rather, the film itself may have failed to be effective in its approach. Ultimately, true horror is about the emotional and psychological impact it leaves on its audience. A film that does not resonate with certain viewers should not be defended by dismissing their perspectives. Instead, a deeper discussion on what makes horror effective should be encouraged. Horror is an evolving genre that thrives on innovation and diverse storytelling techniques. If anything, those who insist that a specific film is the epitome of horror while rejecting all dissenting opinions may be the ones who truly do not understand horror at all.
